honest: Felt scattered but productive across design, code, and prep.
summary: Prepped for a tech interview while iterating on AI product design. Pushed code directly to main instead of branching.
lessons: Dedicated failure slides build interview credibility.
Sidecar UIs keep core products uncluttered.
Small first actions beat complex onboarding.
Pushing directly to main saves time but skips review.
Seed plans must generalize beyond niche use cases.

items.title: interviewPrep
items.body: I was trying to create a 14-slide presentation for a 45-minute project deep dive interview based on the IDG revamp tech design.
decisions: Used dark navy/teal color palette with amber accents
Structured slides to match interview format: problem → architecture → implementation → evaluation → impact
Added dedicated "Failures, Tradeoffs & Lessons" slide for self-awareness
Varied layouts with two-column, comparison tables, stat callouts, and 2x3 grids
Included version timeline and consumer grid for technical depth

items.title: Fetch yage newsletter
items.body: Fetch the latest newsletter from yage-ai.kit.com and save it to my SOP folder.
decisions: Ran the fetch script from the specified directory
Script successfully discovered and downloaded new posts
Converted posts to markdown format
Saved to newsletters/yage-ai/ directory

items.title: Check files.zip for next task
items.body: I wanted to refine the AI goal coach product design based on our session discussion.
decisions: Shape B as the main UI with AI coach as a sidecar
Use forms for onboarding, not chat
Start users with a small first action before showing full plan
Keep one primary plan with optional background habits
Generalize the seed plan to be universally health-positive
open: Need to write the actual PRD document

Need to implement the plan schema and data migration
